name: Chain of Thought
description: Ask the model to reason step by step before producing the final answer. Improves performance on multi-step reasoning.
when_to_use: Use when the task involves math, logic, multi-step reasoning, or non-obvious decomposition.
snippet: Before answering, reason step-by-step inside <thinking></thinking> tags. Then give the final answer in <answer></answer>.

name: Few-Shot Examples
description: Provide 2-5 input/output examples that demonstrate the desired behavior. Dramatically improves format adherence.
when_to_use: Use when the output format is specific or unusual, or when instructions alone are insufficient.
snippet:
Here are examples of good outputs:
<example><input>...</input><output>...</output></example>

name: Self-Critique Loop
description: After producing an answer, ask the model to critique it as if it were a hostile reviewer, then revise. Boosts quality on open-ended tasks.
when_to_use: Use on high-stakes open-ended outputs: essays, designs, strategies.
snippet: First draft your answer. Then critique it as a hostile expert reviewer. Then produce a revised final version that addresses the critique.

name: Role Assignment
description: Assign a specific expert role to the model rather than asking generically. Grounds the response in domain knowledge.
when_to_use: Use when the task benefits from domain expertise (technical, creative, analytical).
snippet: You are a senior [specific role] with [N] years of experience in [specific domain].

name: XML Tag Structuring
description: Wrap different parts of the prompt (context, task, examples, constraints) in XML tags. Claude models particularly are trained on XML and respect it strongly.
when_to_use: Use for any prompt longer than a few sentences or with multiple distinct sections.

name: Output Prefilling
description: Start the assistant's response for it (e.g., with '{' to force JSON). Extremely effective format control.
when_to_use: Use when you need strict output format (JSON, specific template). Most effective via API.

name: Explicit Uncertainty Permission
description: Tell the model it is allowed — encouraged — to say 'I don't know'. Measurably reduces hallucination.
when_to_use: Use on factual, research, or high-stakes tasks where being wrong is worse than being incomplete.
snippet: If you are uncertain about any claim, say so explicitly. It is better to admit you don't know than to guess.

name: Negative Examples
description: Show examples of WRONG outputs alongside right ones. Narrows the error space.
when_to_use: Use when the model consistently makes a specific type of mistake despite positive instructions.

name: Least-to-Most Decomposition
description: Instruct the model to first list sub-problems, then solve them in order, then integrate. Helps on complex multi-part tasks.
when_to_use: Use when the task has clearly separable sub-problems.
snippet: First list the sub-problems. Then solve each one in order. Finally integrate into the complete answer.

name: Confidence Tagging
description: Require the model to tag each non-trivial claim with [fact], [inference], [opinion], or [assume]. Forces epistemic honesty.
when_to_use: Use on research, analysis, and fact-heavy outputs.

name: Audience Specification
description: State exactly who will read the output. Controls tone, depth, and vocabulary.
when_to_use: Always — but especially for writing, explanations, and persuasion.
snippet: The reader is: [specific description — role, expertise level, what they care about].

name: Stop Conditions
description: Tell the model when to stop and ask instead of pushing forward. Prevents hallucinated over-commitment.
when_to_use: Use on tasks where bad assumptions compound (multi-step code, strategic decisions).
snippet: Stop and ask me if: (1) critical info is missing, (2) two paths have very different consequences.
